coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles as effective carrier of gambogic acid for inhibiting VX2 tumor cells
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Yuxia
Yang, Yuxiang
Wang, Xinxin
Yuan, Hongming
Yang, Yuxing
Li, Na
Ni, Chaoying
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports a dual-functional platform that can be used for synergistic therapy, including drug release and magnetic targeting. The platform is realized through an amide reaction between high-fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CD) prepared by microwave method and amino-functional Fe3O4@SiO2 with high saturation magnetic strength and a core-shell structure to obtain multifunctional magnetic fluorescent nanocomposite particles Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs. The structure, drug release behavior, cytotoxicity, fluorescence and distribution in vivo of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles were studied. The results show that the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs with a dynamic diameter of about 155.0 nm and a magnetic saturation intensity of 31.2 emu/g has a higher loading of gambogic acid (GA), and is more conducive to drug release in an environment of pH = 5.7. In addition, Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has low cytotoxicity and can enter VX2 cells. However, GA-loaded Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has an inhibitory effect on VX2 cells, and the cell survival rate is less than 20% at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. Magnetic targeting experiments show that bifunctional magnetic nanoparticles have an outstanding magnetic targeting effect on tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic nanoparticles
Fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Magnetic targeting
Tumor therapy
Drug sustained release

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs loading and sustained release of gambogic acid
In vitro cell experiments of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs
Drug loading and sustained release of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles
In Vitro cytotoxicity and cell uptake of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles
In Vivo studies of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 20577010 , 20971043 ), and the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) nanoparticles controlled drug release.
TEM of a) Fe3O4, b) Fe3O4@SiO2, c) CDs, d) Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs and e) Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs.
Magnetization hysteresis curves of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs.
PL emission spectra of CDs (a) and Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(b).

(a) Drug loading rate of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs to GA in different ratios (b) Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs and GA before loading and (c) after loading for 24 h at pH = 5.7 (d) Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs and GA before loading and (e) after loading for 24 h at pH = 7.4.
Sustained release rate of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs to GA under different pH.

Toxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs on the mouse peritoneal macrophages under different concentration gradients (a)；Toxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (b), Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(GA) (c), and GA (d) on VX2 cells under different concentration gradients.
Fluorescent microscopy images: (a) Nucleus of VX2 cells (b) Incubation of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs in VX2 cells (c) Fig. a and b are merged.
Comparison of in vivo biodistribution between the magnetic targeting group and no magnetic targeting group.
a, b and c are three-visceral tissue sections of the control group e, d and f are three-visceral tissue sections injected with Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs.
(a) Growth of VX2 tumors after various treatments (b) Tumor picture (c) VX2 cell transplanted tumor section.

Sectional view of 18-FDG-PET / CT imaging of tumor-bearing nude mice after injection of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(GA) for 3 and 5 days. (a), after injection of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(GA) for 3 days (b), after injection of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(GA) for 5 days (c), NT group (d), Free Re-188 group.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles as effective carrier of gambogic acid for inhibiting VX2 tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

East China University of Science & Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports a dual-functional platform that can be used for synergistic therapy, including drug release and magnetic targeting. The platform is realized through an amide reaction between high-fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CD) prepared by microwave method and amino-functional Fe3O4@SiO2 with high saturation magnetic strength and a core-shell structure to obtain multifunctional magnetic fluorescent nanocomposite particles Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs. The structure, drug release behavior, cytotoxicity, fluorescence and distribution in vivo of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles were studied. The results show that the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs with a dynamic diameter of about 155.0 nm and a magnetic saturation intensity of 31.2 emu/g has a higher loading of gambogic acid (GA), and is more conducive to drug release in an environment of pH = 5.7. In addition, Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has low cytotoxicity and can enter VX2 cells. However, GA-loaded Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has an inhibitory effect on VX2 cells, and the cell survival rate is less than 20% at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. Magnetic targeting experiments show that bifunctional magnetic nanoparticles have an outstanding magnetic targeting effect on tumors.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, cancer prevention, diagnosis, and treatment remain to be enormous challenges. Most of the drugs, especially anti-tumor drugs, are severely cytotoxic not only to targeted tumor cells, but also normal cells. One of the biggest challenges in nanotechnology-based tumor-targeted therapy is to achieve a sufficiently high concentration of drug in the tumor site while ensuring that other healthy tissues are not affected or the effect is inconsequential to health. Compared with other nanoparticles, Fe3O4 has the advantage of excellent biocompatibility, high saturation magnetization, low toxicity, adjustable structure and function, simple preparation process, and low cost. In recent years, nano-Fe3O4 particles have been widely used in biomedicine, such as targeted drug carriers [14], magnetic resonance imaging [15], biological separation [16], immobilized enzymes [17], and immunoassays [18]. Silica can form nanocomposites with different nanoparticles benefiting from its rich surface hydroxyl groups as a coating material and can improve the stability and biocompatibility of magnetic nanomaterials [1]. Abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of SiO2 are conducive to further functionalize composite particles [19,20]. Many studies have demonstrated the advantages of using magnetic nanoparticles for drug delivery. For example, MNPs combined with methotrexate (MTX) (a therapeutic drug) can target cancer cells [21]. These magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) show higher uptake in cancer cells compared to healthy cells since MTX can be released from nanoparticles through lower pH and intracellular enzymatic environment to induce apoptosis of cancer cells. Hyeon and colleagues [2] reported for the first time the synthesis of magnetic fluorescent composite particles with core-shell structure. In their strategy, mononuclear Fe3O4 and semiconductor particles were encapsulated in mesoporous silica shells [3]. Shi and colleagues [4] grafted high-density carboxyl groups on the pore surface of mesoporous silica nanoparticles to serve as effective ligands for the linkages of Pt atoms from the CDDP anticancer drug. Those nanoparticles showed a pH-responsive and greatly sustained release. It was also reported that the adjustment of the interaction of nanomaterials with drugs by grafting high-density carboxyl groups is critical to drug loading and release. In recent years, by using single-molecule fluorescence microscopy, Brauchle and colleagues [5] observed the diffusion of single-molecule doxorubicin (DOX) in mesoporous silica with different pore sizes and structures. The results indicated that pore size and material functionalized with carboxyl groups can control carrier-drug interaction and drug release kinetics.

Gambogic acid, as an anticancer drug, mainly reduces cancer cell proliferation by inducing cancer cell apoptosis and increases reactive oxygen accumulation to induce autophagy [22,23]. Compared with normal cell lines, cancer cells were found to be very sensitive to GA, which may be due to differences in redox homeostasis [24]. Peng and colleagues [25] studied the cytotoxicity of GA to NK/T cell lymphoma (ENKTL), and found that GA can inhibit cell growth and effectively induce apoptosis of ENKTL cells in vitro by adjusting the time and concentration. According to previous studies, GA has significant inhibitory effects on human liver cancer SMMC-7721 cells, human gastric cancer MGC-803 cells and BGC-823 cells. In experimental studies involving patients with malignant tumors, it is concluded that GA has good safety and has a high control rate for cancer. However, the water solubility of gambogic acid is poor and its solubility in water is found only 2 mg/mL. Therefore, hydrophilic nanoparticles may be designed as drug carriers and delivery systems for gambogic acid to increase drug absorption and improve drug bioavailability [31].

As a new fluorescent marker material, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) are less toxic than semiconductor quantum dots and have better brightness and photostability than traditional organic dyes. In addition, the cost of mass production of CDs is relatively low. Therefore, CDs have good prospects in biosensing [6], bioimaging [7] and drug delivery [8]. Hu et al. [9] creatively proposed the synthesis of CDs by laser method in one step. The graphite powders were suspended in an organic solvent and irradiated by laser. The synthesis and surface modification of CDs were then completed. Fluorescence is originated from the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the CDs. Chiu and colleagues [26] synthesized a magnetic fluorescent carbon quantum dot (GdNS@CQDs) doped with S, N and Gd by using a one-pot microwave method, conjugated with folic acid and loaded doxorubicin to form FA-GdNS@CQDs-DOX, which has excellent fluorescence and magnetic properties and can be used for targeted dual-mode fluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) imaging. The results of drug release experiments show that FA-GdNS@CQDs has a large amount of doxorubicin loading (about 80%) and pH-sensitive drug release. Nanomaterials with different luminescent properties can be prepared by selecting a suitable solvent.

In this study, CDs-terminated magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs) were prepared. The sustained-release drug gambogic acid was loaded with Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs. Moreover, the performance, sustained-release at different pH with different mechanisms, and the cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs were investigated by MTT cell experiments. The intracellular fluorescence was also detected by laser confocal microscope. Under the action of an external magnetic field, magnetic composite nanomaterials actively targeted tumor tissues in an organized manner.

Chemicals used in the experiments are as follows: ferric chloride, sodium acetate, ethylene glycol, diethylene glycol, absolute ethanol, tetraethyl orthosilicate, concentrated ammonia, urea, folic acid, APTES, toluene, EDC, NHS, ascorbic acid, chlorine (all of the above are from National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), Garcinic Acid (Nanjing Jingzhu Biotechnology Co., Ltd.), PBS phosphate buffer (Wuhan Dr. Bioengineering Co., Ltd.), MTT (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), RPMI- 1640 medium (Invitrogen, USA), embryonic bovine serum (Hyclone, USA), and sodium perrhenate injection (customized by Changhai Hospital). All chemicals were of analytical-reagent grade and used without further purification.

A Tecnai-12 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 120 kV was used to characterize the particle morphology and size. The hydration diameter of the nanoparticles was measured by a laser scattering particle size analyzer. D/max 2550 VB/PC type X-ray diffractometer (Kα line of copper target, λ = 0.15406 nm) was used for measurement at room temperature. Magna-IR 550 infrared spectrometer was used for collecting FTIR spectra. VSM BHS-55 vibrating sample magnetometer was used to determine the magnetic properties of the sample. Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer was used to determine the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra. The FV 1000 confocal laser microscope was used to obtain confocal images.

Nano-Fe3O4 was prepared by a solvothermal one-step process. The procedure is as follows: ferric chloride hexahydrate (0.7 g) and anhydrous sodium acetate (1.8 g) were dissolved in a mixture of 20 mL ethylene glycol and 1 mL of diethylene glycol with stirring. The resulting solution was poured into a Teflon lined autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature and washed three times with deionized water and absolute ethanol. The product was then dried in vacuum at 50 °C for 6 h.

The outer layer of Fe3O4 was coated with SiO2 using the classical Stöber method. The procedure is as follows: Fe3O4 nanoparticles (50 mg) was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and 80 mL of absolute ethanol, and then the mixture was sonicated for 30 min and transferred to a 250 mL round bottom flask. 2.0 mL of 28 wt% concentrated ammonia water and 1 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate TEOS were added and mechanically stirred at 30 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was complete, the reaction liquid changed from black to light gray, an indication that the silica had coated on the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles. Then the light gray product was magnetically separated and washed for three times with deionized water and absolute ethanol before being dried in vacuum at 50 °C for 6 h.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared by a one-step microwave method. Urea (2 g) and folic acid (2 g) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, then transferred to a 250 mL round bottom flask and reacted in an 800 W microwave reactor for 8 min. During this process, the solution turned from yellow to brown and finally to dark brown. This process indicated carbon quantum dots was produced. Afterwards, the macromolecular material was filtered off with a disposable aqueous microfiltration membrane 0.22 um, and the purified carbon quantum dots were obtained by dialysis for 24 h with a dialysis bag MD 25–3.5 at a molecular weight cutoff of 3500. After freeze-drying for 24 h, a yellow powder of carbon quantum dots was obtained.

100 mg Fe3O4@SiO2 magnetic nanoparticles were weighed into 60 mL of anhydrous toluene, sonicated for 30 min, then 1 mL of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane APTES was added and refluxed at 120 °C for 12 h. The product is separated with a magnet and the anhydrous toluene is recovered, the product was washed three times with deionized water and absolute ethanol and then dried in vacuum at 50 °C for 8 h. In the above way, aminated magnetic particles Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 was obtained. 100 mg of aminated Fe3O4@SiO2 was dispersed in 100 mL of double-distilled water and dispersed uniformly by ultrasound. EDC (10 mg) and NHS (8 mg) was added and activated for 2 h, and then 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 mL CDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL) was added and shaken at 30 °C for 24 h. The product was then magnetically separated, washed three times and lyophilized for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs loading and sustained release of gambogic acid
ce:section.ce:para: 10.0 mg of gambogic acid was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and then the solution was made up to volume with a 100 mL volumetric flask. 0.2 mL, 0.4 mL, 0.6 mL, 0.8 mL, 1 mL, 2 mL, 4 mL of gambogic acid solution were removed from the volumetric flask with a pipette, and measured at 360 nm with an ultraviolet spectrophotometer for absorbance.

Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles (10 mg) were dissolved in 30 mL of PBS pH = 5.7 and pH = 7.4 respectively and dispersed uniformly for 30 min. 2.00 mg, 2.50 mg, 3.33 mg, 5.00 mg, 10.00 mg of gambogic acid were added respectively in the solution and shaken at 150 rpm for 24 h at 37 °C in a bench-top oscillator. The product was magnetically separated and then washed with a phosphate buffer solution corresponding to desired pH value. 1 mL of the supernatant and the last washing solution were taken separately, and the absorbance at a wavelength of 360 nm was measured with an ultraviolet spectrophotometer to obtain a standard curve. Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles with 3.3 mg of gambogic acid added in the previous step were selected and dispersed in 30 mL of PBS corresponding to pH, and then oscillated in a constant temperature tabletop shaker at 37 °C at 150 rpm. The supernatant was taken at 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, 60 h, and measure the absorbance at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell experiments of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer VX2 cells were cultured in Gibco's medium (DMEM) and supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% double-antibody. Cells were cultured at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere.

After the logarithmic VX2 cells were digested with trypsin, an appropriate amount was used to calculate with counting board and cell suspension concentration was adjusted to 5 × 104 /mL. 100 μL of the cell suspension was added to each well such that the density of the cells to be tested was 5000/well (the edge wells were filled with sterile PBS). The inoculated cell culture plate was placed in an incubator and the bottom of the well is covered with a single layer of cells (96-well flat bottom plate). Nanoparticle samples with a concentration gradient (0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 100 μg/mL) were added and each well was added 100 mL of the sample with six replicate wells per group. After incubation for 5 or 24 h at 37 °C, the culture solution was aspirated and washed once with PBS buffer. 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 0.5% MTT) was then added to each well, and the culture was continued for 4 h. The MTT solution was aspirated and the background was measured. 150 μL of formazan lysate (DMSO) was added and was shaken at low speed for 10 min with a thermostatic shaker and then the absorbance was measure. In the same way, mouse peritoneal macrophages were cultivated, a control group was set up, and the mouse peritoneal macrophages were incubated with different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 100 μg/mL) of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs for 12 or 24 h for research.

The VX-2 cells were placed in a confocal dish and cultured for 12 h, and the culture medium was suck out. 1.0 mL of 50 μg/mL Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanosystem solution in culture medium was added into the confocal dish to incubate the VX-2 cells for 4 h. Afterwards, the culture medium was suck out again, the incubated VX-2 cells was washed with PBS buffer solution for twice, and incubated with 1 mL of culture medium and Red Dot1 dye solution for 10 min. Finally, the incubated VX-2 cells were washed with PBS buffer solution thrice, dropped by a drop of anti-fluorescence quencher, and it was then observed under a laser confocal microscope.

Radioactive element 99mTc-labeled Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(GA) composite particles are prepared by stannous chloride reduction method. 40 mg/mL (solvent is deionized water) ascorbic acid solution and 60 mg/mL stannous chloride solution (solvent is 1 M HCl) were configured. Then, 20 mL ascorbic acid solution and 20 mL stannous chloride solution were added into 10 mL solution with Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles (1 mg/mL). After ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, radioactive 0.1 mL Na99mTcO4 (about 5 mCi) was added, and then marked for 1 h in a water bath at 30 °C. After the reaction was completed, the product was repeatedly washed with physiological saline until the radioactivity of the washing liquid was 0. Finally, the solution was concentrated to about 0.5 mL.

Four long-term ICR mice were cultured in Shanghai Changhai Hospital and divided into two groups. 0.1 mCi of the labeled composite particle solution was injected into 4 ICR mice by tail vein injection. One group was magnetically targeted for 1.5 h in the abdomen of mice by using a medical pulsed magnetic field generator (YMC-11 type), but the other group was not targeted. Then, 4 mice were dissected, and the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, stomach, brain, large intestine, small intestine, blood, bone were weighed, and the distribution of 99mTc-labeled Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) in each organ was detected by a gamma counter.

Eight ICR mice were divided into two groups. One group was not treated as a control group and the other was an experimental group. 100 μg/mL Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs was injected into the mice of the experimental group by tail vein injection and the growth status of the mice was observed. After 14 days, the mice were sacrificed and the liver, spleen, and lung were dissected out. The samples were randomly selected for sectioning and stained with hematoxylin-eosin dye. The tissues and organs of the mice were observed for damages caused by the samples.

VX2 subcutaneously transplanted tumor mice was cultured by Shanghai Changhai Hospital. The inoculation site was selected from the posterior scapula of the axilla of the nude mice (the blood supply was abundant, which was convenient for observation and measurement), and then tumor-bearing mice with similar tumor volume and body weight was obtained. The VX-2 tumor-bearing nude mice were divided into three groups. One group of tail veins was injected with 100 μL Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) (0.5 mg/mL, dissolved in normal saline) every day and then targeted to the tumor for 2 h. One group of tail veins was injected with 100 μL Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs every other day, and one group was not treated as a control group. The normal daily diet of the mice was ensured during the experiment, and tumor volume before administration in the tumor-bearing nude mice was measured as the initial tumor volume V0. The long diameter and short diameter of the tumor site in the tumor-bearing nude mice were measured with vernier calipers every 24 h. The formula of calculating tumor volume was V = (length×width2)/2. The formula for calculating relative tumor volume is V/V0 (V0 is the tumor volume at the start of treatment).

The experimental data are expressed by Mean ± SE, using Origin 7.0 statistical analysis software for data processing, and using F test and t-test to analyze the difference between the measured values of the experimental group and the control group, *A represents the significant difference in data between the experimental group and the control group, P < 0.05(*) is the significant difference, P < 0 0.01 (**) is very significant difference.

Fig. 1 shows the preparation of a multifunctional magnetic fluorescent nanocomposite Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs with high saturation magnetic strength. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared and then Fe3O4@SiO2 was obtained by coating organic mesoporous silica. Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanocomposites was prepared by the amide reaction between highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CD) and amino-functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 [35,36].

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared using a solvothermal method. The average particle size of the nanoparticles was around 125 nm (Fig. 2(a)). As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the spherical Fe3O4 was coated with silica to form a clear core-shell structured Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The silica coating shell was around 12 nm, shown in Fig. 2(d). With increasing magnification, many small black dots with a diameter of 2–3 nm densely dispersed in the shell of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs, as seen in the up-right inset of Fig. 2(d), indicating that there are a large number of CDs. Fig. 2 (c) shows a HRTEM image of CDs, the CDs were uniformly dispersed with a average particle size about 2.5 nm. A lattice fringe with a pitch of 0.206 nm was observed in the HRTEM, which corresponded to the (102) crystal plane of the carbon dot (sp2) (Fig. 2(e)). It was confirmed that the SiO2 shell was encapsulated with CDs.

In addition, the SAED analysis showed that the crystal lattice spacing was 2.50 Å, 2.99 Å and 2.10 Å, respectively, for the (311, 220, 400) crystal planes of Fe3O4 nanocrystals (Fig. S1(a)). These data indicated that the core of the sample was nano-Fe3O4. The SAED of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs sample showed a relatively complete and continuous ring pattern (Fig. S1(b)). This pattern indicated that the CDs outside the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs were fine-grained crystals. Fig. S2(a) shows the average hydration diameter of Fe3O4 to be approximately 126.4 nm. Fig. S2 shows that the average hydration diameter of Fe3O4@SiO2 was about 151.4 nm. The average hydration diameter of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs was about 155.0 nm, due to the grafting of carbon quantum dots onto the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2. A slight increase in hydration diameter is observed.

In order to determine the preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles, XRD, Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were used in this study. The XRD results showed obvious characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 at 2θ = 30.06°, 35.42°, 36.96°, 43.02°, 53.38°, 56.84°, 62.48° (Fig. S3).According to the result, the crystal structure of Fe3O4 magnetic particles did not change after coating SiO2 and grafted carbon quantum dots. That also suggested the stability of the crystal phase. According to Fig. S4, compared with the infrared spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2, that of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs shows new absorption peaks at 1841 cm−1 (CO telescopic vibration) and 1680 cm−1 (-NH-CO- characteristic absorption peak), which was caused by the amide reaction of the amino group of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 with the carboxyl group of the carbon quantum dot. Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs was obtained by grafting Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 on carbon quantum dots. By X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the chemical element composition of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs, the peaks of Si, C, N and O elements can be observed. By splitting the C 1 s, O 1 s, and Si 2p peaks of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs, the spectrum obtained is shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen from the C 1 s spectrum that the carbon atoms in Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs mainly exist in the form of three functional groups (Fig. 3a), the first peak of binding energy at 285 eV is attributable to the CC bond, the second peak at 286.6 eV may be carbon atoms in CO, and the third peak may be attributed to the carbon atom at 288.7 eV connected to the nitrogen atom in OCN, which indicates that the shell contains CDs. The O 1 s spectrum can be fitted to the two main peaks shown in Fig. 3 (c). The first peak at 532.5 eV is attributable to the SiO bond in silica, the second peak at 531.8 eV may be the oxygen atom in the CO bond. At the same time, the Si 2p spectrum is fitted to the two main peaks shown in Fig. 3 (d). The first peak of binding energy at 102.8 eV is related to the SiO bond, and the second peak at 102.5 eV is related to the SiN bond, further indicating that the nanoparticle contains SiO2 and the surface of the shell is grafted with CDs.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the saturation magnetization of the nanomagnetic Fe3O4 reached 55.5 emu·g−1. Correspondingly, compared with the saturation magnetization of Fe3O4@SiO2 (34.6 emu/g), the reduction of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs is very small, with the saturation magnetization at 31.2 emu/g. This is because carbon quantum dots with small particle diameters were grafted on the surface of spherical materials and had less magnetic shielding against Fe3O4 [10]. The coercive force Hc of the three materials was close to 0 Oe, so the prepared magnetic particles were superparamagnetic nanomaterials with high saturation magnetization. When the external magnetic field was guided, the magnetic nanoparticles were targeted to the tumor site and released the drug slowly after the magnetic field was removed.

From Fig. S5(b) of the Zeta potential diagram of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs, it can be found that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has the positive zeta potential value at 37.71 mV. The negatively charged CDs amide bonds reduced the positive charge value and reduced the toxicity to the cells [11]. The dispersion system of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs particles was stable and facilitated the entry into the cells.

In Fig. 5 (a), for the fluorescence spectra of carbon quantum dots, the maximum excitation wavelength of the carbon quantum dot solution is 370 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is 445 nm. Carbon quantum dot aqueous solution has strong fluorescence characteristics. The CDs aqueous solution was irradiated under an ultraviolet lamp, and it was observed that the CDs aqueous solution exhibited obvious blue fluorescence (Fig. 5a).

The fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs was significantly lower than that of carbon quantum dots (Fig. 5(b)), due to the coordination of Fe3+ with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. As a result, transfer of electrons and energy occurred [12], and in turn making CDs a certain degree of fluorescence quenched. Due to the shielding effect of SiO2 on Fe3+, although the fluorescence of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs was weaker than that of CDs, it still maintained a certain level of fluorescence intensity. Such intensity was sufficient for subsequent cell development experiments. The maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs are at 370 nm and 445 nm, respectively, which are the same as those of carbon quantum dots. It can be confirmed that the CDs had been successfully immobilized on the surface of the nano Fe3O4@SiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and sustained release of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs as a carrier has a higher loading (306.7 mg/g) of gambogic acid (GA). Fig. 6 (a) shows that at pH = 5.7 and pH = 7.4, the encapsulation efficiency of the carrier to GA under different carrier/GA ratios, the F test method showed P < 0.01, and there was a very significant difference between the average of each group, namely different carrier/GA ratios cause changes in the encapsulation rate of the carrier to GA. It can be seen in Fig. 6(a) that the optimum carrier/GA ratio is 3: 1, and the encapsulation efficiency of GA is higher than 48%. In addition, the pH value also has an influence on the encapsulation rate. The encapsulation rate under the environment of pH = 5.7 is lower than that of pH = 7.4, regardless of carrier/GA ratio. Under neutral environment (pH = 7.4), the load-bearing capacity of the carrier for GA is higher than that under acidic environment (pH = 5.7). This is because Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles are positively charged, while in a neutral environment (pH = 7.4), gambogic acid has a negative charge due to the carboxyl group and hydroxyl group on the surface, leading to strong electrostatic physical adsorption. In an acidic environment (pH = 5.7), the nanoparticles have a strong positive charge [32], while the gambogic acid is also positively charged, due to the protonation of the 8-hydroxyl group on gambogic acid. Thus, there exists the electrostatic repulsion between the drug and the nanoparticles, which leads to the electrostatic adsorption between them being weakened [33,34]. The findings suggest that an acidic environment leads to the decrease of both drug loading and encapsulation efficiency of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles.

At the same time, in order to further prove that the nanoparticles were successfully loaded with GA and GA did not cause precipitation under different environmental conditions, we observed the photos of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs and GA before and after loading for 24 h under different pH conditions, the color of the solution became obviously lighter, indicating that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs successfully loaded GA.

In vitro simulation experiments for Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs to load and release gambogic acid (GA) in PBS (pH = 7.4 or 5.7) were carried out. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . The GA drug released shows an upward trend during the initial period of 0–12 h, and then gradually becomes placid within the time range of 12–60 h. It is noted that the curve of GA drug release at pH of 5.7 is above the curve of that at pH of 7.4. The former has the maximum amount of GA drug release at 68%, while the latter at 35%. This indicated that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs sustained-release gambogic acid has higher pH sensitivity, which is more favorable for drug release in a slightly acidic environment. Therefore, the amount of gambogic acid released in tumor tissues in a more acidic environment is relatively large. This would greatly improve the treatment efficiency and reduce the damage to normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro cytotoxicity and cell uptake of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the dual-functional magnetic nanoparticles including Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs and gambogic acid-loaded Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs-GA was further determined by MTT cell assay in this paper. The concentration of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs of 0 mol/mL was set as the control group, and the other concentrations were the experimental group. The findings are shown in Fig. 8 (b). Under different concentration gradients, the cell survival rate of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs reached more than 86% after 12 h and 24 h, and the cell survival rate of the experimental group was lower than that of the control group at different times and there is a very significant difference. By increasing the material concentration, there was little difference in the cell survival rate, indicating that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has low cytotoxicity. At the same time, a control group was set up to study the in vitro cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs on mouse peritoneal macrophages. As shown in Fig. 8(a), even if the sample concentration reached 100 μg/mL, the survival score was still as high as 92.58% within 12 h and as high as 91.58% within 24 h, indicating that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs maintain significant low cytotoxicity to mouse peritoneal macrophages. In addition, the survival rate of cells incubated with different concentrations of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs at different times was lower than that of the control group, with extremely significant differences.

Fig. 8(c) shows that the survival rate of cells incubated with different concentrations of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs(GA) at different times was lower than that of the control group, with very significant differences. And as the material concentration increases, the cell survival rate of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) decreases sharply after 12 h and 24 h under different concentration gradients, indicating that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) can indeed reduce cell survival rate. When the concentration of material reached 100 μg/mL, the cell survival rate is less than 20%. The low survival rate indicated that GA can enter the cells through the endocytosis of the carrier, and release the drug in the cell. The drug released in the cell has pharmacological activity, thereby inducing the death of the cell. Fig. 8(d) shows that with the increase of GA concentration, the cell survival rate of GA drops sharply after 12 h and 24 h under different concentration gradients, indicating that GA can reduce cell survival rate, but compared to Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) on cell survival, the impact of the rate is weak.

Cell uptake experiments were performed by incubating VX2 cells with Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 9 (c), blue fluorescence signals from CDs in Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles can be observed near the nucleus, indicating that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles enter VX2 cells via endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vivo studies of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In order to track the in vivo distribution of nanoparticles after drug loading and the corresponding effect of magnetic targeting on distribution, eight ICR mice of similar body weight were selected as animal models. Radioactive 99mTc labeled nanoparticles were injected into the mice. In vivo, one set of magnetic targeting was performed for 2 h with a YMC-11 medical pulsed magnetic field generator, and the other group was naturally placed as a control group. A γ counter was used to measure the radioactivity count of each organ, and the average counts of four mice in each group were taken. The results are shown in Fig. 10 . The intake of radioactivity counts in the liver, spleen and lung were very high. In the non-magnetically targeted group, the intake of liver, spleen and lung reached 37.6%, 21.0% and 14.7%, respectively, while in the magnetically targeted group, the corresponding intake rates reached 41.7%, 27.5% and 16.7%. This difference indicates that the in vivo distribution of nanoparticles has a significant specificity. When it was injected into the body via the vein, it was easily recognized by phagocytic cells, and accumulated in the tissues and organs rich in reticuloendothelial cells [13].

In the two groups of experiments, there is no observable change in the intake of nanoparticles in the heart, brain, large intestine, muscle and bone, but the intake of the magnetic targeting group nanoparticles in the blood was significantly higher than that of the control group. The amount of intake in the lungs, liver and spleen were also significantly higher than that of the control group. This indicates that the nanomagnetic particles of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs entered the blood circulation more quickly and reached the targeted site under magnetic targeting.

As shown in Fig. 10, the nanoparticles are concentrated in the liver, spleen, and lungs in mice. After injecting Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs into ICR mice for a period of time, the liver, spleen, and lungs were taken and combined HE staining. The images in Fig. 11 indicate that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs group of mice liver, lung and spleen did not have serious lesions, suggesting that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs almost cause no damage to normal mouse organs.

Studies have shown that the key factor affecting nanopharmaceutical (metabolic) kinetics and cellular uptake includes the morphology (size and shape) of nanoparticles [27]. The biological distribution, biological clearance, and biocompatibility of nanoparticles in the body are directly affected by its size. Nanoparticles with a particle size of around 100 nm show a longer duration of blood circulation, and the long half-life increases the tendency of nanoparticles to extravasate from blood vessels to tumor blood vessels [28]. Fig. 10 shows that nanoparticles are concentrated in the liver, spleen, and lungs of the mice after intravenous injection. After participating in long-term blood circulation, a large number of nanoparticles are engulfed, digested, and released by monocytes in the liver and spleen. Iron ions are also involved in the circulating metabolism of iron ions in the body [29,30].

In order to further explore the therapeutic effect of gambogic acid-loaded composite nanomaterials on tumors in vivo and also the effects of magnetic targeting, the VX2 tumor-bearing mice were used as a model to evaluate the effect of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) on tumor size. Fig. 12 (b) shows that the difference in tumor size between the PBS control group, the untargeted administration group, and the targeted administration group after 5 days of injection is obvious. From Fig. 12(a), it can be seen that the tumor volume of the magnetically-targeted experimental group decreased significantly after the injection of saline dispersion of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) (0.5 mg/μL)into tumor-bearing mice via their tail vein. After 5 days, the tumor volume was only 40.6% of the initial tumor volume. For the non-magnetic-targeted experimental group, the tumor volume was reduced to 65.6% of initial tumor volume which was higher than that of the magnetic targeting experimental group. The statistical analysis showed that the tumor volume of the mice in the untargeted experimental group and the magnetic targeting experimental group was significantly different from the control group on the second day after injecting drug. This demonstrates that dual-function magnetic nanoparticles have a strong magnetic targeting effect on tumors. Fig. 12(c) shows the HE staining of the tumor section under a 10 × 40 magnification light microscope. It can be seen that the tumor cells are cordlike strips.

The 18-FDG-PET/CT imaging test results further demonstrate the magnetic targeting effect of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) on tumors. After the VX-2 tumor-bearing nude mice group were injected by saline dispersion of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA), with targeting tumor site were treated with external magnetic field for two hours. The treated tumor-bearing nude mice were raised 3 days and 5 days, respectively. In the imaging, the brightness of tumor gradually became darker with time, and was finally similar to that of surrounding tissues (Fig. 13 a and b). However, the imaging for the NT group and the Free 99mTc group still showed very strong tumor brightness (Fig. 13 c and d). This shows that our designed magnetic nano-drugs loaded with GA and 99mTc have therapeutic effects on VX-2 tumors.

Blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) were prepared by microwave method and were grafted to the amino-functionalized core-shell structured of Fe3O4@SiO2 by amide reaction, forming Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs, a fluorescent and drug-loaded bifunctional composite nanocomposite material. Its dynamic diameter was about 155.0 nm and its saturated magnetization was 31.2 emu/g. Due to the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, the nanocomposite particles can take higher loading of gambogic acid (GA). It was found that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs has a pH dependence on the releasing of GA. The environment of pH = 5.7 was more beneficial to the drug release, reaching 68%. In comparison, the drug release reached 35% under an environment of pH = 7.4. Confocal laser scanning microscopy and MTT cell experiment showed that Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs had low cytotoxicity, and the nanoparticles were able to enter VX2 cells via endocytosis. However, Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) loaded with GA had an inhibitory effect on VX2 cells. The cell survival rate was less than 20% at a concentration of 100 μg/mL, indicating that the drug released by GA in cells had pharmacological activity. We also used VX2 tumor-bearing mice as animal models to study the magnetic targeting effect of Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs (GA) on tumors. The tumor volume in the magnetic targeting experimental group had an obvious downward trend, indicating that the dual-functional magnetic nanoparticles had a strong magnetic targeting effect on tumors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (20577010, 20971043), and the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.114783.
